review_summary: Textual grounding is sufficient for concept-focused questions, and no separate video clip is needed.

question_bank.question: In language-model pretraining, what task is the model trained to perform?
choices: Predict the next token from the preceding tokens.
Predict whether two sentences are paraphrases using labeled pairs.
Map each word to an UNK token before training.
Classify every token as a noun, verb, or adjective.
question_bank.explanation: Autoregressive language modeling factors text into next-token prediction problems. The model learns from prefixes and is trained to assign high probability to the observed next token.

question_bank.question: What is the standard pretrain–fine-tune workflow?
choices: Learn broadly from large unlabeled text, then adapt the same parameters on a smaller labeled task.
Train only on labeled task data, then discard the trained parameters.
Pretrain on the downstream labels, then fine-tune on random text.
Use a hand-built vocabulary and never update the model weights.
question_bank.explanation: Pretraining gives the model general linguistic and world knowledge from abundant text. Fine-tuning then specializes those parameters to a task with fewer labeled examples.

question_bank.question: Why is unlabeled text especially valuable for pretraining?
choices: It is far more abundant than labeled task data, so it can support broad representation learning.
It removes the need for any loss function during training.
It guarantees the model will never overfit during fine-tuning.
It makes every downstream task use the exact same evaluation metric.
question_bank.explanation: Raw text supplies enormous numbers of self-supervised prediction examples. This lets the model absorb general structure before labeled downstream data is used.

question_bank.question: Why is perplexity useful during language-model pretraining even if it is not the final goal?
choices: It is an efficient proxy for how well the model predicts text and may correlate with harder downstream evaluations.
It directly measures fairness, factuality, and every downstream skill.
It proves that no fine-tuning will be needed.
It evaluates only the size of the vocabulary, not the model predictions.
question_bank.explanation: Perplexity tracks next-token prediction quality, which is cheap to measure during training. It is not a complete evaluation, but it can indicate whether pretraining is improving.

question_bank.question: Why can fine-tuning from pretrained weights outperform training the same architecture from random initialization?
choices: The optimizer starts near parameters that already encode useful language structure.
Pretrained weights make the downstream loss convex for every task.
Fine-tuning prevents gradients from changing the model.
Random initialization always uses too many parameters.
question_bank.explanation: Pretraining provides a strong starting point. Fine-tuning still performs gradient updates, but it begins from weights that already capture useful regularities rather than from noise.
